content.text: What Is Conditional Branching?

Conditional branching routes execution to different nodes based on the current state. Rather than always following a fixed sequence, the graph reads a field in the state and picks the next node accordingly. This pattern is essential whenever different input types require different processing — classify a document, then hand it to the specialist node for that document type.

attrs.alt: Conditional branching diagram: classify_doc node feeds into a router diamond that routes to legal, financial, technical, or default handler nodes

content.text: add_conditional_edges and Router Functions

In LangGraph, conditional branching is wired with add_conditional_edges(). The second argument is a router function that takes the state and returns a string naming the next node. The function is called after the source node completes.
The router function
route_by_type
is a pure Python function — no LLM call, no side effects, just a dict lookup. Keep router functions this way: they run on every invocation, so slowness or errors here affect every request. The
Literal
return type annotation documents the valid branch names and enables static analysis tools to flag unhandled cases.
LLM-Driven Routing with Structured Output

Sometimes the branching decision is itself a reasoning task that requires the model. The pattern is: run a structured-output LLM call in a dedicated classification node, write the decision to state, then route in a pure router function that reads that field.

Separating classification (LLM call) from routing (pure function) is deliberate. The router can be unit-tested without mocking an LLM. The classification node can be swapped for a rules-based version without touching the graph wiring. Mixing classification logic into the router function makes both harder to test and debug.
Loops via Conditional Edges
Conditional edges can point backwards to an earlier node, creating a loop. The canonical use case is an evaluate-and-revise cycle: draft, review, and either revise (loop) or finalise (exit).
The
revision_count >= 3

guard is mandatory. Without it a loop can run indefinitely if the reviewer never approves. Set the limit explicitly and log when it is hit — reaching the limit silently is a signal that either the draft quality or the review criteria need adjustment.
Multi-Level Routing
Complex pipelines often require nested routing: route by category first, then by urgency within each category. Each level is a separate add_conditional_edges call on a separate node.
Two separate nodes
legal_urgency
and
financial_urgency
both call the same
assess_urgency
function — this is legal in LangGraph because the node names are distinct even though they share an implementation. This avoids duplicating function code while keeping the graph topology explicit.
Router Design Rules
Rule
Reason
Router returns strings, not booleans
LangGraph maps return values to node names; a bool would require a separate mapping layer
Every possible return value must be a registered node
An unmapped value raises KeyError at runtime; document and test all branches
Keep router functions pure
No LLM calls, no I/O; classification belongs in a separate node that writes to state
Add a default/fallback branch
Handles unexpected classifier outputs without crashing the graph
Guard loops with a counter
Prevents infinite loops when the exit condition is never reached
Testing Conditional Branches
Test the router function in isolation first, then test the full graph with a controlled classifier output.
Test every branch explicitly.
Don't rely on one happy-path test. Each branch has unique logic that can fail independently.
Test the fallback branch.
Pass an unrecognised classifier output and assert the graph routes to the default handler rather than raising.
Assert all router return values map to nodes.
This catches regressions when a node is renamed but the router is not updated.
Summary

Conditional branching uses add_conditional_edges() to route execution to different nodes based on state. Router functions are pure Python — classify in a separate node, read the result in the router. Loops are conditional edges that point backwards, always guarded by a counter. Multi-level routing composes naturally: each level is a separate conditional edge on a separate node. Test the router function in isolation and assert every possible return value maps to a real node.
